segments.text: Welcome to Robotics Daily, I'm your host.
On this Wednesday, July 21st, 2026, we've got six exciting updates for you.
From a game-changer in LiDAR mapping to brain-controlled robot tech and four new robots from AGIBOT, it's shaping up to be an interesting day.
From Planet ROS, "ROS Discourse General: OnSLAM: Run LiDAR-inertial mapping on ROS1 bags directly from Windows".

The open-source application OnSLAM has successfully integrated into the AI/tech landscape, enabling users to run LiDAR-inertial odometry and mapping on ROS1 bag files directly from a Windows environment.
This innovative solution simplifies the process of inspecting datasets without requiring a complete Linux and ROS setup.
By utilizing OnSLAM, users can automatically detect compatible sensors, visualize their trajectory and point-cloud map in a browser, and export PLY and PCD files for easy dataset inspection.

The project's creators are now encouraging users to test the application with various sensors, particularly those from Livox, Ouster, Velodyne, or Hesai, to further refine its capabilities and gather valuable feedback.
From The Robot Report, "BrainCo demonstrates brain-controlled robot AI platform."
BrainCo has unveiled a groundbreaking brain-controlled robot AI platform that enables users to direct robots through neural signals in under 200 milliseconds.
This innovation leverages EEG data from human operators to capture both physical actions and brain intent, bridging the gap between physical and cognitive interfaces.
The technology allows robots to perform tasks with precision, such as grasping objects.
BrainCo's Embodied AI Data Collection Solution addresses a critical need for high-quality training data in robotics by converting neural signals into actionable commands.
This development promises to revolutionize the way humans interact with robots.
From Robotics & Automation News, "AGIBOT unveils four new robots at WAIC 2026 as it expands industrial embodied AI portfolio."

AGIBOT has announced the launch of four new robotic products at the World Automation Convention (WAIC) 2026, significantly expanding its portfolio of humanoid robots and industrial automation systems.

The new additions include the AGIBOT A3 Ultra humanoid robot, which features advanced artificial intelligence and machine learning capabilities, as well as a dexterous robotic hand called OmniHand 3 Ultra-M.

Additionally, AGIBOT unveiled the X2 EDU education platform, designed to provide interactive and immersive learning experiences for students, and the G2 Max industrial robot, optimized for precision and efficiency in manufacturing processes.
These new launches are expected to further solidify AGIBOT's position as a leader in the development of embodied AI solutions.

The launch of these new robots is part of AGIBOT's ongoing efforts to integrate artificial intelligence into its product portfolio, enabling users to create more intelligent and autonomous industrial systems.
By expanding its offerings, AGIBOT aims to meet the growing demand for advanced robotic solutions in industries such as manufacturing, education, and healthcare.

The company's strategic expansion plans are expected to drive innovation in the AI and robotics sector, with significant implications for businesses and organizations looking to adopt cutting-edge technologies.
The introduction of these new robots marks an exciting development in the world of embodied AI, where machines are designed to interact with their environment in a more human-like manner.
With its expanding portfolio, AGIBOT is poised to play a key role in shaping the future of robotics and automation, driving innovation and adoption across various industries.
From The Robot Report, "Building robots for unpredictable, infrastructure-free environments,"
a new phase of autonomous robotics is emerging as companies like Burro Robotics adapt to real-world conditions.

Robots designed for controlled lab settings are often unable to function reliably in outdoor agricultural environments, highlighting the need for engineers to focus on tolerance and environmental variability.
Burro Robotics has learned about these challenges through hands-on experience in its operations, leading to a new approach that prioritizes infrastructure-free localization and perception.
By focusing on developing robots capable of adapting to diverse conditions, companies can unlock significant advancements in autonomous robotics for industrial environments.
Research investment in this area is expected to yield substantial benefits.

The success of Burro Robotics suggests that the next phase of autonomous robotics will prioritize environmental variability and real-world deployment, leading to more reliable and effective robots in challenging outdoor settings.
From Planet ROS, "ROS Discourse General: PyZeROS: a Python-first alternative to `rclpy` over Zenoh."
PyZeROS is an experimental Python-first alternative to `rclpy`, offering minimal dependencies and no ROS installation required.
It can install through pip and communicates with ROS 2 through Zenoh, interoperating with standard ROS 2 nodes using rmw_zenoh_cpp.
PyZeROS is designed for asyncio and asynchronous Python, providing custom ROS messages defined directly with Python classes.
This new alternative was made possible by leveraging the capabilities of asyncio executor and Zenoh to create a seamless integration between ROS 2 and Python-based systems.
PyZeROS achieves this through a combination of asynchronous programming and direct integration with ROS 2's rmw_zenoh_cpp library, allowing users to define custom messages directly in Python classes.
This approach eliminates the need for traditional ROS message definitions, making it an attractive option for developers seeking to streamline their workflows.
The development team has effectively utilized pip installation and asyncio executor capabilities to create a lightweight yet feature-rich solution.
The emergence of PyZeROS is significant, as it offers a more accessible entry point for Python developers into the world of ROS 2 programming.

With its focus on minimal dependencies and ease of use, PyZeROS has the potential to bridge the gap between the Python and ROS communities, facilitating greater collaboration and innovation in the field of robotics and AI.
From NVIDIA Developer Blog, "Integrate NVIDIA Omniverse RTX Sensor Simulation Into Existing Apps."

This story is about NVIDIA's latest development in integrating its Omniverse libraries, which allows developers to seamlessly integrate real-time, physically grounded sensor simulations from OpenUSD scenes into existing application stacks.
NVIDIA's omniverse libraries provide modular APIs, including ovrtx, that enable developers to simulate RTX-based sensor outputs such as cameras, lidar, and radar.

This is achieved through the use of lightweight C and Python SDKs, which allow for configuration of render and sensor outputs, step simulation from the app loop, and retrieval of output tensors on CPU or GPU.
By integrating these capabilities, developers can create more realistic and immersive experiences in their applications.

This integration is poised to significantly enhance the AI and tech industries by providing developers with a versatile toolset for simulating real-world sensor data, paving the way for further innovation in fields such as autonomous vehicles, robotics, and virtual reality.
"Alright, gearheads!
That's all for today's robotic rebellion - may the code be with you!
Thanks for joining me on this wild ride into the world of robotics.
Until tomorrow, keep on coding and stay SLAMmin'!"
